title: End of module: Moments and Equilibrium of Rigid Bodies

questions.text: A force of $F = 15\,\text{N}$ acts perpendicular to a lever at a distance $d = 0.6\,\text{m}$ from the pivot. Find the moment of the force about the pivot.
A force of $40\,\text{N}$ is applied perpendicular to a spanner at a perpendicular distance of $0.25\,\text{m}$ from a nut. Find the moment of the force about the nut.
What is the SI unit of the moment of a force?
questions.explanation: Moment is force multiplied by perpendicular distance, so its unit is the newton metre, $\text{N}\,\text{m}$. (Note this is dimensionally the same combination as the joule, but moments are not energies, so N m — not J — is used.)

questions.text: A seesaw is pivoted at its centre. Child A weighs $300\,\text{N}$ and sits $1.5\,\text{m}$ from the pivot. Child B sits $2.5\,\text{m}$ from the pivot on the other side. What must child B's weight be for the seesaw to balance?
questions.explanation: By the principle of moments, clockwise moment = anticlockwise moment: $300 \times 1.5 = W_B \times 2.5$, so $W_B = \dfrac{450}{2.5} = 180\,\text{N}$.

questions.text: A uniform rod of length $3\,\text{m}$ and weight $60\,\text{N}$ is hinged at one end and held horizontal by a vertical force $F$ applied at the other end. Find $F$.
questions.explanation: The rod is uniform, so its weight acts at its midpoint, $1.5\,\text{m}$ from the hinge. Taking moments about the hinge: $F \times 3 = 60 \times 1.5 = 90$, so $F = \dfrac{90}{3} = 30\,\text{N}$.

questions.text: For a uniform rod, where does its weight act when modelling moments?
choices: At the end nearest any applied load
At the midpoint of the rod
Weight is spread out and cannot be modelled as a single force
questions.explanation: A uniform rod has constant mass per unit length, so its centre of mass — and hence the single point through which its weight acts — is at its midpoint.

questions.text: A uniform beam $AB$ of length $5\,\text{m}$ and weight $100\,\text{N}$ rests on supports at $A$ ($x=0$) and $B$ ($x=5$). An additional load of $50\,\text{N}$ is placed at $x = 4\,\text{m}$ from $A$. Find the reaction at $A$.
questions.explanation: Taking moments about $B$ (distance $5\,\text{m}$ away) eliminates the reaction at $B$: the beam's weight acts at its midpoint, $2.5\,\text{m}$ from $A$, i.e. $5-2.5=2.5\,\text{m}$ from $B$; the load is $5-4=1\,\text{m}$ from $B$. So $R_A \times 5 = 100 \times 2.5 + 50 \times 1 = 250 + 50 = 300$, giving $R_A = \dfrac{300}{5} = 60\,\text{N}$.

questions.text: Which statement correctly gives the principle of moments for a rigid body in equilibrium?
choices: The sum of all forces acting on the body is zero
The sum of clockwise moments about any point equals the sum of anticlockwise moments about that same point
The moment about the centre of mass is always greater than the moment about any other point
Moments only need to balance about the body's actual physical pivot or hinge
questions.explanation: The principle of moments states that for a body in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments about any chosen point equals the sum of anticlockwise moments about that same point — and this holds for every choice of point, not only a physical hinge.

questions.text: A uniform ladder of weight $200\,\text{N}$ and length $5\,\text{m}$ rests with its foot on rough ground, $3\,\text{m}$ horizontally from a smooth vertical wall, and its top touching the wall $4\,\text{m}$ above the ground. Find the normal reaction from the wall, $N_B$.
questions.explanation: The weight acts at the midpoint, a horizontal distance $1.5\,\text{m}$ from the foot; the wall's horizontal force $N_B$ acts at height $4\,\text{m}$. Taking moments about the foot: $N_B \times 4 = 200 \times 1.5 = 300$, so $N_B = \dfrac{300}{4} = 75\,\text{N}$.

questions.text: For the same ladder as the previous question ($N_B = 75\,\text{N}$, weight $200\,\text{N}$, smooth wall), find the minimum coefficient of friction $\mu$ between the ladder and the ground needed to prevent slipping.
questions.explanation: Vertical equilibrium gives $N_A = W = 200\,\text{N}$ (the smooth wall contributes no vertical force). Horizontal equilibrium gives $F_A = N_B = 75\,\text{N}$. Equilibrium requires $F_A \le \mu N_A$, so $\mu \ge \dfrac{75}{200} = 0.375$.

questions.text: A moment of $18\,\text{N}\,\text{m}$ is required to loosen a nut. If a mechanic can apply at most $60\,\text{N}$ of force perpendicular to a spanner, find the minimum perpendicular distance from the nut at which the force must be applied.
